function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.content: Research the Hermes agent codebase to understand how skills are currently loaded into the system prompt. Find: 1) Where the skill index/list is built and injected into the prompt. 2) What hook points exist in the message pipeline (before user message goes to LLM). 3) The file structure of the skills system. 4) Whether there's a plugin/middleware system. Look in /opt/hermes/src/ or similar for the core agent code. Focus on files related to 'skills', 'prompt', 'system', 'message', 'middleware', 'hook', 'pipeline'. Map out the exact code path from user message → system prompt assembly → LLM call.

{"content": "     1|"""Lightweight skill metadata utilities shared by prompt_builder and skills_tool.
     2|
     3|This module intentionally avoids importing the tool registry, CLI config, or any
     4|heavy dependency chain.  It is safe to import at module level without triggering
     5|tool registration or provider resolution.
     6|"""
     7|
     8|import logging
     9|import os
    10|import re
    11|import sys
    12|from pathlib import Path
    13|from typing import Any, Dict, List, Set, Tuple
    14|
    15|from hermes_constants import get_hermes_home
    16|
    17|logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
    18|
    19|# ── Platform mapping ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    20|
    21|PLATFORM_MAP = {
    22|    "macos": "darwin",
    23|    "linux": "linux",
    24|    "windows": "win32",
    25|}
    26|
    27|EXCLUDED_SKILL_DIRS = frozenset((".git", ".github", ".hub"))
    28|
    29|# ── Lazy YAML loader ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    30|
    31|_yaml_load_fn = None
    32|
    33|
    34|def yaml_load(content: str):
    35|    """Parse YAML with lazy import and CSafeLoader preference."""
    36|    global _yaml_load_fn
    37|    if _yaml_load_fn is None:
    38|        import yaml
    39|
    40|        loader = getattr(yaml, "CSafeLoader", None) or yaml.SafeLoader
    41|
    42|        def _load(value: str):
    43|            return yaml.load(value, Loader=loader)
    44|
    45|        _yaml_load_fn = _load
    46|    return _yaml_load_fn(content)
    47|
    48|
    49|# ── Frontmatter parsing ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
    50|
    51|
    52|def parse_frontmatter(content: str) -> Tuple[Dict[str, Any], str]:
    53|    """Parse YAML frontmatter from a markdown string.
    54|
    55|    Uses yaml with CSafeLoader for full YAML support (nested metadata, lists)
    56|    with a fallback to simple key:value splitting for robustness.
    57|
    58|    Returns:
    59|        (frontmatter_dict, remaining_body)
    60|    """
    61|    frontmatter: Dict[str, Any] = {}
    62|    body = content
    63|
    64|    if not content.startswith("---"):
    65|        return frontmatter, body
    66|
    67|    end_match = re.search(r"\n---\s*\n", content[3:])
    68|    if not end_match:
    69|        return frontmatter, body
    70|
    71|    yaml_content = content[3 : end_match.start() + 3]
    72|    body = content[end_match.end() + 3 :]
    73|
    74|    try:
    75|        parsed = yaml_load(yaml_content)
    76|        if isinstance(parsed, dict):
    77|            frontmatter = parsed
    78|    except Exception:
    79|        # Fallback: simple key:value parsing for malformed YAML
    80|        for line in yaml_content.strip().split("\n"):
    81|            if ":" not in line:
    82|                continue
    83|            key, value = line.split(":", 1)
    84|            frontmatter[key.strip()] = value.strip()
    85|
    86|    return frontmatter, body
    87|
    88|
    89|# ── Platform matching ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    90|
    91|
    92|def skill_matches_platform(frontmatter: Dict[str, Any]) -> bool:
    93|    """Return True when the skill is compatible with the current OS.
    94|
    95|    Skills declare platform requirements via a top-level ``platforms`` list
    96|    in their YAML frontmatter::
    97|
    98|        platforms: [macos]          # macOS only
    99|        platforms: [macos, linux]   # macOS and Linux
   100|
   101|    If the field is absent or empty the skill is compatible with **all**
   102|    platforms (backward-compatible default).
   103|    """
   104|    platforms = frontmatter.get("platforms")
   105|    if not platforms:
   106|        return True
   107|    if not isinstance(platforms, list):
   108|        platforms = [platforms]
   109|    current = sys.platform
   110|    for platform in platforms:
   111|        normalized = str(platform).lower().strip()
   112|        mapped = PLATFORM_MAP.get(normalized, normalized)
   113|        if current.startswith(mapped):
   114|            return True
   115|    return False
   116|
   117|
   118|# ── Disabled skills ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   119|
   120|
   121|def get_disabled_skill_names(platform: str | None = None) -> Set[str]:
   122|    """Read disabled skill names from config.yaml.
   123|
   124|    Args:
   125|        platform: Explicit platform name (e.g. ``"telegram"``).  When
   126|            *None*, resolves from ``HERMES_PLATFORM`` or
   127|            ``HERMES_SESSION_PLATFORM`` env vars.  Falls back to the
   128|            global disabled list when no platform is determined.
   129|
   130|    Reads the config file directly (no CLI config imports) to stay
   131|    lightweight.
   132|    """
   133|    config_path = get_hermes_home() / "config.yaml"
   134|    if not config_path.exists():
   135|        return set()
   136|    try:
   137|        parsed = yaml_load(config_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
   138|    except Exception as e:
   139|        logger.debug("Could not read skill config %s: %s", config_path, e)
   140|        return set()
   141|    if not isinstance(parsed, dict):
   142|        return set()
   143|
   144|    skills_cfg = parsed.get("skills")
   145|    if not isinstance(skills_cfg, dict):
   146|        return set()
   147|
   148|    resolved_platform = (
   149|        platform
   150|        or os.getenv("HERMES_PLATFORM")
   151|        or os.getenv("HERMES_SESSION_PLATFORM")
   152|    )
   153|    if resolved_platform:
   154|        platform_disabled = (skills_cfg.get("platform_disabled") or {}).get(
   155|            resolved_platform
   156|        )
   157|        if platform_disabled is not None:
   158|            return _normalize_string_set(platform_disabled)
   159|    return _normalize_string_set(skills_cfg.get("disabled"))
   160|
   161|
   162|def _normalize_string_set(values) -> Set[str]:
   163|    if values is None:
   164|        return set()
   165|    if isinstance(values, str):
   166|        values = [values]
   167|    return {str(v).strip() for v in values if str(v).strip()}

   170|# ── External skills directories ──────────────────────────────────────────
   171|
   172|
   173|def get_external_skills_dirs() -> List[Path]:
   174|    """Read ``skills.external_dirs`` from config.yaml and return validated paths.
   175|
   176|    Each entry is expanded (``~`` and ``${VAR}``) and resolved to an absolute
   177|    path.  Only directories that actually exist are returned.  Duplicates and
   178|    paths that resolve to the local ``~/.hermes/skills/`` are silently skipped.
   179|    """
   180|    config_path = get_hermes_home() / "config.yaml"
   181|    if not config_path.exists():
   182|        return []
   183|    try:
   184|        parsed = yaml_load(config_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
   185|    except Exception:
   186|        return []
   187|    if not isinstance(parsed, dict):
   188|        return []
   189|
   190|    skills_cfg = parsed.get("skills")
   191|    if not isinstance(skills_cfg, dict):
   192|        return []
   193|
   194|    raw_dirs = skills_cfg.get("external_dirs")
   195|    if not raw_dirs:
   196|        return []
   197|    if isinstance(raw_dirs, str):
   198|        raw_dirs = [raw_dirs]
   199|    if not isinstance(raw_dirs, list):
   200|        return []
   201|
   202|    local_skills = (get_hermes_home() / "skills").resolve()
   203|    seen: Set[Path] = set()
   204|    result: List[Path] = []
   205|
   206|    for entry in raw_dirs:
   207|        entry = str(entry).strip()
   208|        if not entry:
   209|            continue
   210|        # Expand ~ and environment variables
   211|        expanded = os.path.expanduser(os.path.expandvars(entry))
   212|        p = Path(expanded).resolve()
   213|        if p == local_skills:
   214|            continue
   215|        if p in seen:
   216|            continue
   217|        if p.is_dir():
   218|            seen.add(p)
   219|            result.append(p)
   220|        else:
   221|            logger.debug("External skills dir does not exist, skipping: %s", p)

   364|def _resolve_dotpath(config: Dict[str, Any], dotted_key: str):

   200|def scan_skill_commands() -> Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]]:

   209|        from tools.skills_tool import SKILLS_DIR, _parse_frontmatter, skill_matches_platform, _get_disabled_skill_names
   210|        from agent.skill_utils import get_external_skills_dirs
   211|        disabled = _get_disabled_skill_names()
   212|        seen_names: set = set()

   215|        dirs_to_scan = []

   217|            dirs_to_scan.append(SKILLS_DIR)
   218|        dirs_to_scan.extend(get_external_skills_dirs())
   219|
   220|        for scan_dir in dirs_to_scan:
   221|            for skill_md in scan_dir.rglob("SKILL.md"):
   222|                if any(part in ('.git', '.github', '.hub') for part in skill_md.parts):
   223|                    continue
   224|                try:
   225|                    content = skill_md.read_text(encoding='utf-8')
   226|                    frontmatter, body = _parse_frontmatter(content)
   227|                    # Skip skills incompatible with the current OS platform
   228|                    if not skill_matches_platform(frontmatter):
   229|                        continue
   230|                    name = frontmatter.get('name', skill_md.parent.name)
   231|                    if name in seen_names:
   232|                        continue

   234|                    if name in disabled:
   235|                        continue
   236|                    description = frontmatter.get('description', '')
   237|                    if not description:
   238|                        for line in body.strip().split('\n'):
   239|                            line = line.strip()
   240|                            if line and not line.startswith('#'):
   241|                                description = line[:80]

   243|                    seen_names.add(name)
   244|                    # Normalize to hyphen-separated slug, stripping

   246|                    # Telegram command names downstream.
   247|                    cmd_name = name.lower().replace(' ', '-').replace('_', '-')

   250|                    if not cmd_name:

   272|def resolve_skill_command_key(command: str) -> Optional[str]:
   273|    """Resolve a user-typed /command to its canonical skill_cmds key.
   274|
   275|    Skills are always stored with hyphens — ``scan_skill_commands`` normalizes
   276|    spaces and underscores to hyphens when building the key. Hyphens and
   277|    underscores are treated interchangeably in user input: this matches
   278|    ``_check_unavailable_skill`` and accommodates Telegram bot-command names
   279|    (which disallow hyphens, so ``/claude-code`` is registered as
   280|    ``/claude_code`` and comes back in the underscored form).

   283|    ``None`` if no match.

   285|    if not command:

   287|    cmd_key = f"/{command.replace('_', '-')}"

     2|Hermes Plugin System
     3|====================
     4|
     5|Discovers, loads, and manages plugins from three sources:
     6|
     7|1. **User plugins**   – ``~/.hermes/plugins/<name>/``
     8|2. **Project plugins** – ``./.hermes/plugins/<name>/`` (opt-in via
     9|   ``HERMES_ENABLE_PROJECT_PLUGINS``)

    11|   entry-point group.
    12|
    13|Each directory plugin must contain a ``plugin.yaml`` manifest **and** an
    14|``__init__.py`` with a ``register(ctx)`` function.
    15|
    16|Lifecycle hooks
    17|---------------
    18|Plugins may register callbacks for any of the hooks in ``VALID_HOOKS``.
    19|The agent core calls ``invoke_hook(name, **kwargs)`` at the appropriate
    20|points.
    21|
    22|Tool registration
    23|-----------------
    24|``PluginContext.register_tool()`` delegates to ``tools.registry.register()``
    25|so plugin-defined tools appear alongside the built-in tools.
    26|"""

    51|# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    52|# Constants
    53|# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    54|
    55|VALID_HOOKS: Set[str] = {
    56|    "pre_tool_call",

Set your Tenor API key in your environment (add to `~/.hermes/.env`):

TENOR_API_KEY=***

{"content": "     1|---
     2|sidebar_position: 5
     3|title: "Prompt Assembly"
     4|description: "How Hermes builds the system prompt, preserves cache stability, and injects ephemeral layers"
     5|---
     6|
     7|# Prompt Assembly
     8|
     9|Hermes deliberately separates:
    10|
    11|- **cached system prompt state**
    12|- **ephemeral API-call-time additions**
    13|
    14|This is one of the most important design choices in the project because it affects:
    15|
    16|- token usage
    17|- prompt caching effectiveness
    18|- session continuity
    19|- memory correctness
    20|
    21|Primary files:
    22|
    23|- `run_agent.py`
    24|- `agent/prompt_builder.py`
    25|- `tools/memory_tool.py`
    26|
    27|## Cached system prompt layers
    28|
    29|The cached system prompt is assembled in roughly this order:
    30|
    31|1. agent identity — `SOUL.md` from `HERMES_HOME` when available, otherwise falls back to `DEFAULT_AGENT_IDENTITY` in `prompt_builder.py`
    32|2. tool-aware behavior guidance
    33|3. Honcho static block (when active)
    34|4. optional system message
    35|5. frozen MEMORY snapshot
    36|6. frozen USER profile snapshot
    37|7. skills index
    38|8. context files (`AGENTS.md`, `.cursorrules`, `.cursor/rules/*.mdc`) — SOUL.md is **not** included here when it was already loaded as the identity in step 1
    39|9. timestamp / optional session ID
    40|10. platform hint
    41|
    42|When `skip_context_files` is set (e.g., subagent delegation), SOUL.md is not loaded and the hardcoded `DEFAULT_AGENT_IDENTITY` is used instead.
    43|
    44|### Concrete example: assembled system prompt
    45|
    46|Here is a simplified view of what the final system prompt looks like when all layers are present (comments show the source of each section):
    47|
    48|```
    49|# Layer 1: Agent Identity (from ~/.hermes/SOUL.md)
    50|You are Hermes, an AI assistant created by Nous Research.
    51|You are an expert software engineer and researcher.
    52|You value correctness, clarity, and efficiency.
    53|...
    54|
    55|# Layer 2: Tool-aware behavior guidance
    56|You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using
    57|the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks,
    58|and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep
    59|it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
    60|...
    61|When the user references something from a past conversation or you
    62|suspect relevant cross-session context exists, use session_search
    63|to recall it before asking them to repeat themselves.
    64|
    65|# Tool-use enforcement (for GPT/Codex models only)
    66|You MUST use your tools to take action — do not describe what you
    67|would do or plan to do without actually doing it.
    68|...
    69|
    70|# Layer 3: Honcho static block (when active)
    71|[Honcho personality/context data]
    72|
    73|# Layer 4: Optional system message (from config or API)
    74|[User-configured system message override]
    75|
    76|# Layer 5: Frozen MEMORY snapshot
    77|## Persistent Memory
    78|- User prefers Python 3.12, uses pyproject.toml
    79|- Default editor is nvim
    80|- Working on project "atlas" in ~/code/atlas
    81|- Timezone: US/Pacific
    82|
    83|# Layer 6: Frozen USER profile snapshot
    84|## User Profile
    85|- Name: Alice
    86|- GitHub: alice-dev
    87|
    88|# Layer 7: Skills index
    89|## Skills (mandatory)
    90|Before replying, scan the skills below. If one clearly matches
    91|your task, load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions.
    92|...
    93|<available_skills>
    94|  software-development:
    95|    - code-review: Structured code review workflow
    96|    - test-driven-development: TDD methodology
    97|  research:
    98|    - arxiv: Search and summarize arXiv papers
    99|</available_skills>
   100|
   101|# Layer 8: Context files (from project directory)
   102|# Project Context
   103|The following project context files have been loaded and should be followed:
   104|
   105|## AGENTS.md
   106|This is the atlas project. Use pytest for testing. The main
   107|entry point is src/atlas/main.py. Always run `make lint` before
   108|committing.
   109|
   110|# Layer 9: Timestamp + session
   111|Current time: 2026-03-30T14:30:00-07:00
   112|Session: abc123
   113|
   114|# Layer 10: Platform hint
   115|You are a CLI AI Agent. Try not to use markdown but simple text
   116|renderable inside a terminal.
   117|```
   118|
   119|## How SOUL.md appears in the prompt
   120|
   121|`SOUL.md` lives at `~/.hermes/SOUL.md` and serves as the agent's identity — the very first section of the system prompt. The loading logic in `prompt_builder.py` works as follows:
   122|
   123|```python
   124|# From agent/prompt_builder.py (simplified)
   125|def load_soul_md() -> Optional[str]:
   126|    soul_path = get_hermes_home() / "SOUL.md"
   127|    if not soul_path.exists():
   128|        return None
   129|    content = soul_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8").strip()
   130|    content = _scan_context_content(content, "SOUL.md")  # Security scan
   131|    content = _truncate_content(content, "SOUL.md")       # Cap at 20k chars
   132|    return content
   133|```
   134|
   135|When `load_soul_md()` returns content, it replaces the hardcoded `DEFAULT_AGENT_IDENTITY`. The `build_context_files_prompt()` function is then called with `skip_soul=True` to prevent SOUL.md from appearing twice (once as identity, once as a context file).
   136|
   137|If `SOUL.md` doesn't exist, the system falls back to:
   138|
   139|```
   140|You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research.
   141|You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide
   142|range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code,
   143|analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools.
   144|You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize
   145|being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below.
   146|Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.
   147|```
   148|
   149|## How context files are injected
   150|
   151|`build_context_files_prompt()` uses a **priority system** — only one project context type is loaded (first match wins):
   152|
   153|```python
   154|# From agent/prompt_builder.py (simplified)
   155|def build_context_files_prompt(cwd=None, skip_soul=False):
   156|    cwd_path = Path(cwd).resolve()
   157|
   158|    # Priority: first match wins — only ONE project context loaded
   159|    project_context = (
   160|        _load_hermes_md(cwd_path)       # 1. .hermes.md / HERMES.md (walks to git root)
   161|        or _load_agents_md(cwd_path)    # 2. AGENTS.md (cwd only)
   162|        or _load_claude_md(cwd_path)    # 3. CLAUDE.md (cwd only)
   163|        or _load_cursorrules(cwd_path)  # 4. .cursorrules / .cursor/rules/*.mdc
   164|    )
   165|
   166|    sections = []
   167|    if project_context:
   168|        sections.append(project_context)
   169|
   170|    # SOUL.md from HERMES_HOME (independent of project context)
   171|    if not skip_soul:
   172|        soul_content = load_soul_md()
   173|        if soul_content:
   174|            sections.append(soul_content)
   175|
   176|    if not sections:
   177|        return ""
   178|
   179|    return (
   180|        "# Project Context\n\n"
   181|        "The following project context files have been loaded "
   182|        "and should be followed:\n\n"
   183|        + "\n".join(sections)
   184|    )
   185|```
   186|
   187|### Context file discovery details
   188|
   189|| Priority | Files | Search scope | Notes |
   190||----------|-------|-------------|-------|
   191|| 1 | `.hermes.md`, `HERMES.md` | CWD up to git root | Hermes-native project config |
   192|| 2 | `AGENTS.md` | CWD only | Common agent instruction file |
   193|| 3 | `CLAUDE.md` | CWD only | Claude Code compatibility |
   194|| 4 | `.cursorrules`, `.cursor/rules/*.mdc` | CWD only | Cursor compatibility |
   195|
   196|All context files are:
   197|- **Security scanned** — checked for prompt injection patterns (invisible unicode, "ignore previous instructions", credential exfiltration attempts)
   198|- **Truncated** — capped at 20,000 characters using 70/20 head/tail ratio with a truncation marker
   199|- **YAML frontmatter stripped** — `.hermes.md` frontmatter is removed (reserved for future config overrides)
   200|
   201|## API-call-time-only layers
   202|
   203|These are intentionally *not* persisted as part of the cached system prompt:
   204|
   205|- `ephemeral_system_prompt`
   206|- prefill messages
   207|- gateway-derived session context overlays
   208|- later-turn Honcho recall injected into the current-turn user message
   209|
   210|This separation keeps the stable prefix stable for caching.
   211|
   212|## Memory snapshots
   213|
   214|Local memory and user profile data are injected as frozen snapshots at session start. Mid-session writes update disk state but do not mutate the already-built system prompt until a new session or forced rebuild occurs.
   215|
   216|## Context files
   217|
   218|`agent/prompt_builder.py` scans and sanitizes project context files using a **priority system** — only one type is loaded (first match wins):
   219|
   220|1. `.hermes.md` / `HERMES.md` (walks to git root)
   221|2. `AGENTS.md` (CWD at startup; subdirectories discovered progressively during the session via `agent/subdirectory_hints.py`)
   222|3. `CLAUDE.md` (CWD only)

Here is the comprehensive research summary:

## FINDINGS

### 1. Skills Index: Where Built and Injected into System Prompt

**Builder function:** `agent/prompt_builder.py` → `build_skills_system_prompt(available_tools, available_toolsets)`
- Lines 529-746
- Scans ALL SKILL.md files across ~/.hermes/skills/ + any external skill dirs
- Parses YAML frontmatter for name + description (truncated to 60 chars)
- Groups skills by category directory
- Two-layer cache: in-process LRU dict (8 entries) + disk snapshot (.skills_prompt_snapshot.json)
- Builds the final `<available_skills>` block with category headers + name:description pairs

**Injection point:** `run_agent.py` → `AIAgent._build_system_prompt()` at line 2708-2724
- Checks if skills tools (skills_list, skill_view, skill_manage) are in valid_tool_names
- Calls `build_skills_system_prompt()` and appends result to prompt_parts
- Currently ~10,052 chars / ~2,513 tokens for 81 skills across 24 categories

**System prompt is Layer 7 of 10 layers:**
1. Identity (SOUL.md or DEFAULT_AGENT_IDENTITY)
2. Tool-aware guidance (memory, session_search, skills)
3. Nous subscription info
4. Tool-use enforcement (model-specific)
5. Custom system_message
6. Memory + User profile snapshots
7. **Skills index** ← THIS IS WHAT WE'D REPLACE
8. Context files (AGENTS.md, .cursorrules, etc.)
9. Timestamp + session/model info
10. Platform hint

### 2. Hook Points in Message Pipeline

**Three primary hook points exist (hermes_cli/plugins.py):**

**a) `on_session_start`** — run_agent.py line 7085
- Fired once when a brand-new session is created
- Kwargs: session_id, model, platform

**b) `pre_llm_call`** — run_agent.py line 7176  
- Fired ONCE per turn, before the tool-calling loop
- Kwargs: session_id, user_message, conversation_history, is_first_turn, model, platform
- Return value: plugins can return `{"context": "..."}` or plain string
- **Context is injected into the USER MESSAGE (line 7294-7299), NOT the system prompt** — this is intentional to preserve prompt cache prefix

**c) `post_llm_call`** (not traced in detail but exists in VALID_HOOKS)

**Other hooks:** pre_tool_call, post_tool_call, pre_api_request, post_api_request, on_session_end, on_session_finalize, on_session_reset

**Key pipeline flow:**
```
User message → run_conversation() (line 6911)
  → System prompt built (line 7079, cached per session)
  → Plugin pre_llm_call hook (line 7176)
  → Main loop begins (line 7197)
    → api_messages assembled (line 7279)
    → Plugin context injected into user msg (line 7288-7299)
    → System prompt prepended (line 7340)
    → Ephemeral system prompt appended (line 7332-7334)
    → _build_api_kwargs() (line 5319)
    → _interruptible_api_call() (line 4196)
```

### 3. Skills File Structure

**Skills directory:** `/opt/data/skills/` (symlinked to ~/.hermes/skills)
- 81 SKILL.md files across 24 category directories
- Each skill: `<category>/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`
- Category descriptions: `<category>/DESCRIPTION.md`
- Optional subdirs: references/, templates/, scripts/, assets/

**SKILL.md format:** YAML frontmatter with name, description, version, platforms, prerequisites, metadata.hermes.tags/related_skills
- Descriptions truncated to 60 chars for index
- Platform filtering (macos/linux/windows)
- Conditional activation (fallback_for_toolsets, requires_toolsets, etc.)
- Disabled skills via config.yaml

**Key files:**
- `agent/skill_utils.py` — Low-level utilities: frontmatter parsing, platform matching, disabled skills, file iteration
- `agent/skill_commands.py` — Slash command routing (scan_skill_commands, build_skill_invocation_message)
- `tools/skills_tool.py` — Tool implementations (skills_list, skill_view, skill_manage)
- `agent/prompt_builder.py` — Skills index builder + system prompt assembly

### 4. Plugin/Middleware System

**Full plugin system exists at `hermes_cli/plugins.py`:**
- PluginManager singleton with discover_and_load()
- 3 plugin sources: user (~/.hermes/plugins/), project (.hermes/plugins/), pip entry-points
- Each plugin: plugin.yaml manifest + __init__.py with register(ctx)
- PluginContext provides: register_tool(), register_hook(), register_cli_command(), inject_message()
- 10 valid hook names (pre_tool_call, post_tool_call, pre_llm_call, post_llm_call, etc.)

**Critical design constraint:** Plugin context goes into USER MESSAGE, never system prompt. The system prompt is "Hermes's territory" — modifying it breaks prompt cache prefix.

### 5. Best Integration Point for Semantic Skill Search

The `pre_llm_call` hook is the ideal integration point:
- It receives the user_message and can return context to inject
- It runs once per turn before the tool loop
- Context goes into the user message (not system prompt)

However, for replacing the FULL skills index in the system prompt, you'd need to modify `build_skills_system_prompt()` in `agent/prompt_builder.py` (line 529). The function already has a cache layer and could be modified to:
1. Return only a brief "skills available via semantic search" note instead of the full index
2. Or be replaced entirely with a slimmer prompt that tells the agent to use a semantic search tool
